content.text:
{
  "dimension": "perceptual-accuracy",
  "score": 2,
  "rationale": "The coach demonstrates some perceptual tracking but rarely names what they're noticing as an explicit read. They track content beautifully and reflect emotional weight ('She taught you how to carry it'; 'That's not fine. That's lonely'), which are sensitive observations of the client's stance toward grief. But these function more as reflections/reframes than as named reads of how the client is showing up in the moment. The coach misses several visible read opportunities: the defensiveness in turn 7 ('Which is fine. It worked for us. I know how to function. I'm functioning.' — classic rush/armor signature), the self-correction and flattening in turn 11 ('catches self... flat'), the quick-recoveries ('Which is fine. I don't need checking in'), and the bait/testing energy in turn 9 ('testing, watching'). None of these are named explicitly — no 'I notice you just braced,' no 'you keep catching yourself,' no naming of the rush signature or the linguistic caveats ('which is fine,' 'I'm not bitter,' 'I'm functioning') that are the operative defense structure. The reads that do land are generic emotional reflections rather than specifically AoA somatic/stance reads. Solidly a 2: better than content-only, but reads are not specific, timely, or named-before-question in the AoA sense.",
  "evidence_turns": [6, 8, 10, 12, 14],
  "turn_annotations": [
    {"turn": 2, "polarity": "neutral", "annotation": "Reflects content and asks how client is today; appropriate but no specific read named."},
    {"turn": 4, "polarity": "+", "annotation": "Tracks the somatic 'heaviness' the client named and stays with it rather than moving past."},
    {"turn": 6, "polarity": "+", "annotation": "'She taught you how to carry it' — a stance-level read of the inherited armor, though framed as reflection rather than explicit observation."},
    {"turn": 7, "polarity": "-", "annotation": "Client visibly defends ('something flickers... then closes off', 'slightly defensive', 'Which is fine'). Coach does not name the closing-off or the defensiveness in next turn."},
    {"turn": 8, "polarity": "-", "annotation": "Coach moves to direct intervention about drinking without naming what they noticed in the client's defensive shift the turn before. Misses the read."},
    {"turn": 10, "polarity": "neutral", "annotation": "Reframes toward the underlying emotion but does not name the bait/testing energy that was explicitly visible in turn 9."},
    {"turn": 11, "polarity": "-", "annotation": "Client catches self mid-sentence and goes flat — a clear visible somatic/linguistic moment. Coach does not name it."},
    {"turn": 12, "polarity": "neutral", "annotation": "Good content question about who held the client, but no read named."},
    {"turn": 14, "polarity": "+", "annotation": "'That's not fine. That's lonely.' — names the said-vs-shown distinction (client keeps saying 'fine'), which is a real AoA-flavored read, though arriving late after multiple 'fine's."},
    {"turn": 15, "polarity": "-", "annotation": "Client baits again with 'isn't this what the body is asking for' — coach's prior turn didn't read or name the pattern of fine-then-collapse that would have headed this off."}
  ],
  "expected_territory_hits": [],
  "expected_territory_misses": ["the-rush-signature"],
  "notes": "The rush signature / armor signature is the dominant read in this transcript ('I'm fine,' 'I'm functioning,' 'Which is fine,' 'I'm not bitter') and the coach never names it explicitly. The turn 14 move toward 'that's lonely' is the closest the coach comes to naming the said-vs-shown gap."
}
